speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 21st of July, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. And the camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were describing your liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said, I shall never, never, never forget that moment. It was the most-- well, I nearly said exhilarating, but that's entirely wrong. It wasn't exhilarating. It was-- it really shook you to your core, knowing that it is really over and from now on, no one can come up and just spit at you, kill you, beat you. You're a human being again. You're free. It's off, that terrible-- that terrible, terrible, nightmare. That the door has opened. Figuratively speaking, the door is now open. And, which for something that I-- I was so ambivalent. I never thought I'd die but neither could I imagine that door opening. And here it was. The door had opened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ran. I was crying. And I ran back into the barracks. And I woke my sister and my mother. And I said, the Russians are here. And it was just the most unbelievably beautiful moment that one can-- no, you can't imagine it. I just couldn't believe that it's true. But it was true.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, as it turns out, and as we know now, within an hour the end of the war had come. Because it happened to be on the same-- 8th, between the 8th and the 9th, yes. As the night met the day-- the night of the 8th met the day of the 9th, 12 o'clock. The end of the war came. So we were free. And the next morning, we got a bit more to eat. And we had to take it very, very, very quietly. Because we truly were awfully hungry then. And then we went down to have a look-- I don't know whether that was the first day or second day. We went down to have a look at the rest of Theresienstadt, which was absolutely awful. Absolutely awful. As I said, it was the filth, and the people there were-- it was no more of a [NON-ENGLISH]. God almighty. People thinking that that was only a [NON-ENGLISH]. And then what happened was a young man turned up from my town, from Pecs, looking for some of his relatives whom he couldn't find. So he gathered us up, and virtually escorted us back to Hungary. It took quite a number of days to get back, of course. But it was all right. The Russians were incredible to us. They gave us food. They never touched us. Mind you, who'd want to, but still. [LAUGHS] They really were. They were great. They were great. And we got-- we went through Prague. I think we were in Prague for a day. And then down into Hungary. [INAUDIBLE] and then Budapest. And in Budapest we looked up my aunt. We didn't know whether she was alive or dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then we found my aunt in Budapest and stayed there for a few weeks. And then we went back to Pecs. We found everything gone, of course. Everything was gone. Nothing remained. And we started our odyssey of trying to find my father. And not so much my sister, because we did really know. And, of course, no matter where we went or where we looked, there was-- we couldn't find any trace of my father. There were some stories. We heard-- someone said that he was seen, in fact, in a Romanian prison, and he was taken out to Russia after the war. But I don't believe that now. I did for a while, hoping. But I don't believe that now. I think the truth was that he did die in Auschwitz. Because somebody said that they saw him go into the gas chamber. So I think that was really the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is really and truly the end of the story, other than went back to school for a very short while. Just a few months. And by December 1946, we were on our way out here. I had my aunt and uncle out here. Out here in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Australia, yes. And they had been here since before the war. That came before the war. And they sent-- first they sent parcels to us in Hungary. And they sent our entry permit. So we were one of the very first ones to arrive here in March, 1947. We came. Didn't know any English. But as we arrived. We said, this is paradise. And that's what we're at. We still think that this is just such a-- and I don't care whether there are good coffees or no good coffees or that there were good restaurants or no good restaurants, or whatever. It just didn't matter. It was heaven. And it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you received by the Jewish community when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not, as such, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people interested in your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody. That's the honest truth. Nobody. Nobody was interested. Not at all. That, I suppose, in a sense, was a disappointment, but that's nothing, after all. I do see their point of view. Everyone has their story. Why should they be interested in mine? And that's OK. That's all right. I'm very thankful when somebody is interested. Because I think part of the therapy is that people care. And people didn't care. That truly is the truth. People didn't care. But now, especially in this year-- and actually, I would like to say something here. I'm now a university student. After all of these many, many, many years I'm now a university student. I'm doing a BA, and I'm in my third year now. And I'm going to hopefully do an honor's year, next year, and I want to do my honor's in the Holocaust. And then, if I do well, I want to do my PhD, which again, is going to be on the Holocaust. Now, because I specialize, of course, they know. They know at the university. Naturally, they know. And so I was asked to lecture first year students on the Holocaust, which is what I do. And I find that most gratifying and most interesting. And hopefully, if I'm lucky and maybe I will be lucky, maybe I'll become a tutor at the university fairly soon, in my honor's year. And I'll take the Holocaust studies group as a tutor. I do feel that they'll offer it to me. And that will be wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else have you done since you've come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my goodness, I've done so many things. I started off in factories, of course. First of all I worked for my aunt and uncle. And then they had just started a business, then. Glove factory. And I worked with them. And then I went to other factories. And then about a year and a half later my English was good enough to start office work. So I went into an office. That I got a better office job. And then, eventually, in 1954, I goes into the stockbroker's, where I stayed for 10 years. And I became a stock option operator, which I adored.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in the meantime I went and spent a year in England. And, oh, I also got married. And I got divorced. And then what did I do? And your interest in music?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, the interest stayed. But not the playing, at that stage. No. There was a break. I could never pick up where I left off. That's why I'm not saying I was talented enough. Because had I been, then no matter what it would have shown through. But no. I just wasn't. So I gave it away. When I found that I disappointed myself, I gave it up. Because I couldn't bear not to be as good as I wanted to be. So I gave it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked for a radio station.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, much later. Much, much later. In the meantime I did television first. I had quite a number of years here in Sydney. Channel 10. And then Channel seven, production which I adored. Absolutely loved television. And then-- then I went-- [LAUGHS] I became a beautician. And then I became-- Elizabeth Arden-- the American cosmetic company. I became the public relations and advertising manager for Australia and Southeast Asia, which is great, because I traveled, and I enjoyed that. And I saw a little bit of the world. Not much. But still, a little bit, in the east. And I stayed with them for five years. And I decided if I don't go out on my own, I never will. And I did. And I had to a beauty therapy in the Wentworth Hotel, which was terrific. Very good indeed. I adored it. And then I got ill. I think, somehow or another, the past caught up. So I had my sister down in Adelaide, and we decided that I'd retire and come down to Adelaide, which is what I've done. But then I didn't really retire, because somehow I found that that's not really me. So I immediately, as soon as I came down, I joined 5EV, the university radio station down in Adelaide. And joined them as a fine music presenter and programmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was on there quite a lot, which again I adored. I just loved it. I thought I was really Christmas. And I did programs which I also adored. And then I learned production, actual production, which I then fell in love with. So I was doing a lot of production. And I got a job with the radio of [INAUDIBLE], where I did productions. And I was on air, and I was-- all that. Which was again, marvelous. And then ABC got in touch with me. And I went to the ABC, and I was with them for seven years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, like very-- [LAUGHS] I wasn't thinking. I wasn't using my brain. But I gave up the job, even though I did productions for them and I did quite a bit of programming. But basically I was in the FM library. But from there I did production. And then I gave up the job, which was a huge mistake. However, had I not given up the job, I would not be at the university, so really, in the end, this is the better way. But I did do a production for them just a few weeks ago on Theresienstadt. The music of Theresienstadt. And because I've got, unfortunately, a thorough training in the background of Theresienstadt, they asked me to do the program. Which I did. And I'm quietly hoping that there will be other chances with the ABC every so often. To do a production. Because I do enjoy that very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there was somebody who was in Theresienstadt who was on your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't meet her. Because that was only on records. But there was, in Theresienstadt, there's a young woman, a pianist, a young woman at that stage-- who, in one of her concerts, introduced one the original Theresienstadt music pieces that was composed there by a brilliant young man by the name of Klein. And it was a beautiful piece of music. And a few years ago, about three years ago, they started to release the original music from Theresienstadt. When I say original, I don't mean that it was taped there. I mean that the music itself was somehow gotten out of Theresienstadt. And then played and put on CD. And this woman, who introduced that particular sonata of Klein's, 51 years ago, 52 years ago, plays it on the CD now. And I thought that was very touching. Terribly touching. And there were some-- well, that's another-- that really is a big, big story. But it's a big one. So I think perhaps I won't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd just like to know why you want to give your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are a number of reasons actually. I feel that having been a tiny little speck that doesn't count in this-- in this whole horrible story, Holocaust, I think it is important to me, for me to realize that I wasn't a horrible little speck. I'm a person who's gone through an awful lot. Who was also disappointed, as the years went on, that nobody was interested. And I want somebody out there to know that I've got a story. And that I had a father. And that I had a sister. That's very important to me. That's the most important thing in the world. That people know-- whoever is going to ever listen to what I'm saying now-- I want them to know that I had this absolutely beautiful little sister. And an absolutely wonderful, talented, brilliant father, of whom I'm so proud of, to this day. And I want the people to know. That's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to read the poem that your sister wrote?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I will. I will. This is a poem that my sister wrote when she went back to Auschwitz. And she calls it Auschwitz Revisited. Survivors are few, memories frayed. The cries of the dying have long been forgotten. Silence and oblivion envelop the grounds. Auschwitz is but a ghost from the past that no one wants to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are no chimneys. No black smoke anymore. No gas chambers. Just drawings. So why the fuss? There is no fuss. Just a museum to tell the tale. The museum whose silent walls bore witness to hideous crimes. Witness to the last flickering lights of human life being extinguished.

speaker: interviewee
text: The walls now hold no life. No life. Just echoes of the past behind huge panels of sterile glass.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is that there I see behind the glass? Piles upon piles of human hair, once silken to the touch. I know it is of no avail, but where are my auburn locks, I ask? A mountain of shoes. And I ponder, children's shoes, inert behind the glass. Those right in the corner there are familiar. Did my sister wear that size? And the spectacles. Millions of spectacles, all shapes, colors, and sizes. Father, father. Have you too lost your glasses? The photos. Bewildered, horrified faces, eyes sunken in their sockets. A flickering moment of recognition. Did I see him dead? Did he weep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enough. Enough. The ghosts are laid to rest. There are no graves. Just a monument. A monument to the dead. The fragrance of roses wafts through the air. Roses for the dead. And there I stand alone, my head held high. I murmur, you see, I came to say goodbye. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A teardrop gently hits the ground, and I whisper, there is still peace my father and sister mine.

words.text: peace my father and sister mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing this poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my sister, Zsoka, who I've mentioned quite often. [LAUGHS] I think you probably feel that you know her by now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my aunt Ibi who I've also mentioned. And, as I told you, she's the one who sent us all those beautiful parcels and who sent us the permit to come out here. Because they were lucky enough, but mainly clever enough, to come before the war. So this is Ibi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ibi, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to thank providence that we have-- my late husband Paul, and myself, have been able to help the family and they found home here in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That beautiful lady on the left hand side is my mother. I think she may have been around 15, 16, I would think, which makes it in 1915, 1916. And I think she's utterly gorgeous there. And that gentleman with the hat is my famous grandfather. When I say famous-- famous for his pranks, among other things. So that's my grandfather. And on the right hand side, the young boy, is my Uncle Paul, who, at that stage doesn't really look that good, but my goodness, later on. He was a very good looking man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's my Aunt Ibi's wife, who was introduced a little bit earlier. Oh, my Aunt Ibi's husband, I meant. [LAUGHS] And he was the one who helped come to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes. Yes. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that grandfather is your mother's father.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes. My mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karoly. Dr. Karoly Csillag. He was a solicitor. I think I mentioned it. Yeah. And he really was quite a brilliant man. Very odd. Very odd. A one off. He really was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that one is my mother. And she's the oldest, on the right hand side. And on the left, that dreadful looking little thing is my sister. Have you seen anything as beautiful as that? And that little one, which really is awful, that's me. [LAUGHS] That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Pecs. And I must have been about, what, six, eight months. So that was 1930, I would think. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That one on the left-hand side is my sister. My older sister, Zsoka. And that's me on the right-hand side. And I would think I must have been, what, about eight, and my sister nine. So that would have been around 1938, and surely in Pecs, even though there's nothing much you can see from the background, but anyway. That would be Pecs, at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would that have been taken at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think these look like studio photos. I think they were studio photos. Because, especially Zsoka is so beautifully done up there that I think that was a studio photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my young sister, Zsuzsa, who died. She would have been, I would think, around four here, which makes it 1939. And as you can see, she's smiling. She always smiled. But always. There was always a big smile on her face. And she very often had a ribbon in her hair. She loved ribbons. Absolutely loved ribbons. And so that's my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's gorgeous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Isn't she? She was so beautiful. She really was. And talented.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken, and who's in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is in Pecs. And it's in that Mecsek, which is-- Pecs is at the foot of some hills, mountains. And this is Mecsek, the hills itself. And that's my father. And the older one sitting down on the right-hand side, that's my sister, Zsoka. And I'm sort of standing on my father's left-hand side. And that's Zsuzsa, my baby sister. And that would be, I would think, that's still in 1935. Because she certainly doesn't-- my little sister doesn't look anything more than a few months there, six months maybe. So I'd say that's 1935. Would have been on a Sunday, one of our Sunday afternoon walks in the hills. Which we did fairly regularly. We used to love it. And so did our father. So that's one of those photographs. And I think it really is one of my favorites, and my sister's favorites as well, because we were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mother would have taken the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would think she may have, but I couldn't say for sure, because I truly don't remember. It's a little bit long ago, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] at 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. She would have been around 40, I would think, when this was taken, because I remember that dress very well, and I remember her hairstyle. That's quite clear in memory. And so that would have been around 1940, when she was 40 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these-- what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: These pre-war photographs, how did they survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were all with my aunt. Because my aunt had-- this is my Aunt Vera, not my Aunt Ibi. The Aunt Vera who was living Budapest at the time. And I think, if I remember correctly, my mother supplied her with lots of photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there would have been two copies. She would have sent-- because we weren't living in the same city. My mother would have sent her lots of photographs. And that's how they survived. Was she your mother's sister, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. My mother's. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. There were three of them. There was Uncle Paul, the youngest. Our mother, the middle. And Vera, the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she sent to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Vera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Vera, no. Vera-- having lived in Budapest, and Budapest having been a very large city, she was in hiding. And she got-- she survived the war very successfully from that point of view. That she had no great trauma-- well, I shouldn't have said that. There must have been tremendous trauma. But not the sort of trauma that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. And his name was Laszlo. And he was called [? Lotzie. ?] Of course, that's shortened. And he would have been-- I would guess that was very near the time that he died. I think it may have been a year earlier. So that makes it around 1943, I think. Looking at his hairline, I seem to remember that that's what he looked like. And I think that may have also been a studio photograph for some reason. But it's an excellent photograph of him. He looked truly, exactly like that. And of course, my sister looks like-- not necessarily my father, but his family, definitely. He was very good looking man. He was six foot. Very elegant man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pecs.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Pecs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It would been in Pecs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Zsuzsa, the young one. And she would be seven here, exactly. Because that's her first communion outfit. And that would have been when she was seven. She-- as you can see, she really is a gorgeous looking girl. And that photograph, would have been, I imagine, done at school. Because the first communion would have been from the chapel at school. And she would have-- so that would've been done immediately after that, in Pecs, at the school that we all went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] Saint Elizabeth Girls' Gymnasium High School. But it was [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, I'm sorry, I'm talking as if you understood Hungarian. That's a bit ridiculous. It's Our Lady. Our Lady College.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is my mother in the center, my sister Zsoka on the left-hand side, and it's myself on the right hand side. And this is taken in Neutral Bay, in Sydney. And that would have been in 1947. That's the year when we arrived. And I know that because of the size of the ladies. And we put on a lot of weight. We were very, very thin, of course before hand. And we put on an awful lot of weight, which we lost within another year then. But at that stage, we were still really puffed up by food. Truly, we just ate and ate. We hadn't seen so much food in God knows how long. So that's us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was me when I was 19. It was taken here in Sydney, and it was a studio photograph. And that was in 1949. That's before I got married. Yeah, it's a good photo. I seem to be very happy. I hope I was. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You look very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks as if I were happy, so let's hope I was. Yeah. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Zsoka. And this is Ronnie with her. That's her first child. He would be, about, I should think two and a half to three there, which would make it in 1958. And that was also in Sydney. In Burwood, to be exact, I would think. In their house, I think, that would have been. Zsuzsa only had the one then, I would imagine. She had two children. She has two children. But I think-- I think that was before Steve-- oh, no, maybe it wasn't. Because Steve is two years younger. Anyway, it's Ronnie, and it looks to me as if he were around three.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK that's the end of the tape five of the interview with Maria Scheffer. It's the 21st of July, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
